items.prompt: Which method is best suited for collecting standardized attitudes from many respondents?
choices: Simple random sampling without an instrument
items.explain: Surveys provide comparable answers at scale, though response bias and low response rates remain watch-outs.

items.prompt: In Example 2.5, what comes before choosing a survey platform?
choices: Defining the instrument and stratified sample plan
items.explain: The Wi-Fi example emphasizes instrument design and building-stratified sampling before any form builder is selected.

items.prompt: Web scraping is appropriate whenever a website has public HTML, regardless of terms of service or layout stability.
items.explain: Scraping raises legal, ethical, and fragility concerns; APIs are preferred when available.

items.prompt: Why might a team combine observation with surveys?
choices: So depth from watching behavior complements breadth from standardized questions
Because observation always generalizes better than surveys
items.explain: Mixed designs pair contextual observation with survey reach when neither method alone answers the question.

items.prompt: In Example 2.6, why must the team re-check selectors after each term?
items.rubric: Layout/HTML changes break scrapers; selectors must be updated.
items.sample_answer: Course catalog page layouts change routinely, which breaks CSS selectors and requires maintenance after each term.
